ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general French toast entry (FOODON_03315091); no specific cheese French toast variant exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers French toast as a bread-based egg-coated dish; cheese topping is not separately coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cheese French toast; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of cheese French toast in Singapore consists of two slices of egg-coated bread pan-fried in butter and sandwiched or topped with a processed cheese slice, weighing approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese French Toast — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020019 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) component data for processed cheese and butter in proportion to recipe composition, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese French toast. GI of 74 is estimated from white bread (GI ~71–75, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle. The egg and cheese components lower the effective GI marginally but the white bread base dominates. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (74 × 21.77 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 19.3 × 0.63 ≈ 12.1 g per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 459 mg/100 g from processed cheese and butter, with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one slice and choose reduced-sodium cheese and unsalted butter to bring sodium below 300 mg per serving; avoid sweet toppings such as condensed milk or syrup which compound cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bread base (GI ~74) with 21.8 g carbohydrates/100 g and only 1.9 g dietary fibre drives rapid postprandial glucose excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should substitute white bread with wholemeal or sourdough bread to lower the glycaemic index, pair with additional protein (e.g. extra egg or lean meat), and avoid sweet toppings to reduce glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~5.2 g/100 g from processed cheese and butter, combined with sodium 459 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit frequency of consumption, replace butter with a plant-based oil such as olive oil, choose lower-fat cheese, and avoid adding high-sugar or high-sodium condiments to keep saturated fat and sodium within daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.2 g/100 g and AGE formation from pan-frying in butter promote pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider oven-baking instead of pan-frying to reduce AGE formation, replace butter with olive oil, and use wholegrain bread to reduce the pro-inflammatory load of this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese French Toast is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack dish consisting of thick slices of white bread dipped in beaten egg and pan-fried or grilled in butter until golden, then served with or sandwiched around a slice of processed cheese. The result is a warm, crispy-edged, soft-centred toast with a savoury-eggy flavour and a mild dairy richness from the melted cheese. It is commonly found at traditional Singaporean kopitiams (coffee shops) and hawker stalls, often served alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea). Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 226 kcal, 12.6 g protein, 9.8 g fat (estimated saturated fat ~5.2 g), and 21.8 g carbohydrates with 1.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 459 mg/100 g due to the processed cheese and salted butter. Calcium is elevated at 325 mg/100 g, reflecting the cheese content. The white bread base gives this dish a high glycaemic index (~74), making it a concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance. The saturated fat and sodium levels also warrant caution for those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension. A healthier preparation would substitute wholemeal bread, use unsalted butter or olive oil, and select reduced-sodium cheese.
common_user_queries: Is cheese French toast suitable for diabetics?
How is cheese French toast made?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020019, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for the known ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, which is well-supported.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB source for this record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for cheese French toast (F05020019) as of the accessed date. Saturated fat of 5.2 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for processed cheese (NDB 01042) and salted butter (NDB 01001) in proportion to the typical recipe composition for this dish; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. GI of 74 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cheese French toast was found in approved sources. The input dietary_tags included 'wholegrain' which is inconsistent with the white bread base described; this tag has been removed. The 'high_protein' tag has been retained as protein at 12.6 g/100 g is above average for bread-based dishes.
